docs.title: TCGC — TACITUS Conflict Grammar Corpus
docs.text:
The TCGC is an open benchmark for evaluating AI systems on conflict-analysis tasks. It measures what generic RAG systems fail at: commitment tracking, causal ordering, interest/position separation, provenance attribution, and narrative drift.

Items are short, structured, and scored against a typed subgraph — not free text. Every extracted primitive must cite its source span.

docs.title: What's in this repo
docs.text: Resource Path Description JSON Schema schema/tcgc-v0.1.json Canonical item shape Sample items items/v0.1-sample/ 5 public items Harness tcgc/ Python package CLI tcgc validate, tcgc score Validation and scoring Adapters tcgc/adapters/ HELM and lm-eval

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Quickstart — run your first evaluation in 5 minutes
  • Task types — all 14 task definitions
  • Domains — 7 conflict domains
  • Metrics — 5 scoring functions
  • Methodology — annotation protocol
  • Submission guide — submit to the leaderboard
  • Leaderboard — current standings
  • Roadmap — what's coming

Homepage: https://www.tacitus.me/research/tcgc

docs.title: Inter-Annotator Agreement
docs.text:
IAA report for v0.1-sample (5 items). Cohen's κ reported per item.

Item Task type Domain Annotators κ tcgc-0001 commitment-tracking workplace A1, A2 0.82 tcgc-0002 event-ordering peace-process A1, A3 0.91 tcgc-0003 contradiction-detection governance A2, A4 0.76 tcgc-0004 interest-extraction commercial A1, A2, A3 0.64 tcgc-0005 provenance-attribution diplomatic A2, A4 0.88

Notes:

  • tcgc-0004 (interest-extraction) has the lowest κ at 0.64. This reflects genuine ambiguity in inferring latent interests from stated positions — a known hard case. Three annotators were used to get a majority signal.
  • tcgc-0002 (event-ordering) has the highest κ at 0.91 because temporal ordering from causal text has low ambiguity once dates are stripped.
  • Full corpus targets: κ ≥ 0.70 for graph-scored tasks. LLM-judged tasks use subjective review.

docs.title: Domains
docs.text: 7 conflict domains. The scope statements below are verbatim from the TCGC specification.

docs.title: workplace
docs.text:
HR grievances, performance disputes, promotion blocks.

Typical documents: Slack/email threads, performance reviews, HR correspondence.

Typical actors: Individual employees, managers, HR representatives, legal counsel.

Conflict archetypes: Commitment-vs-denial of verbal agreement; performance assessment dispute; promotion block with unstated constraint.

docs.title: commercial
docs.text:
Contract breach, vendor dispute, joint-venture friction.

Typical documents: Negotiation transcripts, contracts, email chains, term sheets.

Typical actors: Vendor, buyer, legal teams, procurement.

Conflict archetypes: Position/interest divergence in pricing negotiation; disputed delivery milestone; joint-venture governance deadlock.

docs.title: governance
docs.text:
Board disagreement, mandate conflict, committee deadlock.

Typical documents: Board minutes, committee reports, governance policies.

Typical actors: Board directors, chairs, executives, committee members.

Conflict archetypes: Contradictory minutes on a key decision; mandate dispute between committees; procedural challenge to voting outcome.

docs.title: peace-process
docs.text:
Ceasefire, DDR, political-track multilateral negotiation.

Typical documents: Diplomatic memos, track-II summaries, communiqués.

Typical actors: State delegations, mediators, civil society observers, international bodies.

Conflict archetypes: Disputed event sequence in a ceasefire timeline; commitment revocation in DDR process; framing shift between Track I and Track II.

docs.title: policy
docs.text:
Regulatory contestation, stakeholder reception, public-consultation aftermath.

Typical documents: Policy briefs, consultation submissions, regulatory filings, press releases.

Typical actors: Regulators, industry groups, civil society, government ministries.

Conflict archetypes: Contradictory stakeholder claims on impact; contested timeline of regulatory action; interest/position split in lobbying communication.

docs.title: family
docs.text:
Inheritance, custody arrangement, intergenerational wealth dispute.

Typical documents: Legal correspondence, mediation transcripts, wills, family agreements.

Typical actors: Family members, legal representatives, mediators.

Conflict archetypes: Disputed oral commitment on inheritance; custody arrangement contested after circumstances change; contradictory interpretations of a prior agreement.

docs.title: diplomatic
docs.text:
State-to-state friction, border incident, multilateral block formation.

Typical documents: Press briefings, diplomatic cables, UN statements, multilateral communiqués.

Typical actors: State foreign ministries, diplomatic representatives, multilateral bodies.

Conflict archetypes: Contradictory claims between two states on a bilateral issue; disputed provenance of a statement; block formation around a contested multilateral agenda item.

docs.title: Leaderboard
docs.text:
Current standings on the v0.1-sample (5 items). Updated automatically on merged submissions.

System Status v0.1-sample Full corpus Card TACITUS Dialectica (reference) pending — — — GPT-4-class zero-shot pending — — — Claude 3-class zero-shot pending — — — Strong RAG baseline pending — — — GraphRAG baseline pending — — —

Scores are per-task-type averages. No single headline number.

To add your system: see the Submission Guide.

docs.title: Annotation Protocol
docs.text: TCGC uses a three-pass annotation protocol.

docs.title: Pass 1 — Primitive tagging
docs.text: The lead annotator reads the source material and tags every relevant entity as one of the 8 ACO primitives: actor, claim, interest, constraint, leverage, commitment, event, narrative. Each primitive receives a provenance reference pointing at the source span (message id, document id, or turn:N).

docs.title: Pass 2 — Edge labeling
docs.text: The same annotator labels directed edges between primitives using the 18 ACO edge types. Every edge must carry a provenance reference. Unlabeled edges fail validation.

docs.title: Pass 3 — Ground-truth QA
docs.text: A second annotator reviews the complete item: checks primitive types, edge directions, provenance bindings, and the rubric.scoring assignment. Disagreements are resolved by discussion; persistent disagreements are flagged with a lower inter_annotator_kappa value.

docs.title: IAA Targets
docs.text:
Task type group Target κ Graph-scored (graph_overlap, provenance_f1, contradiction_pair_f1, kendall_tau) ≥ 0.70 LLM-judged (interest-extraction, narrative-drift, position-interest-separation) Subjective review by two annotators; no hard κ floor

IAA values are reported per item in source.inter_annotator_kappa.

docs.title: Bulk Import
docs.text:
To import items into the TACITUS platform for annotation:

  1. Draft source text and question in the TCGC JSON template
  2. Run tcgc validate <item>.json — must exit 0
  3. Submit via the platform API or PR to items/
  4. Assign to two annotators via the platform assignment tool

The scripts/build_manifest.py script regenerates items/manifest.json after any batch import.

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text: 5 scoring functions. Each is bound to specific task types — mismatches are a validation error.

docs.title: graph_overlap
docs.text:
Formula: Weighted mean of node Jaccard and edge Jaccard.

score = 0.4 × node_jaccard + 0.6 × edge_jaccard

Node Jaccard: |gold_nodes ∩ pred_nodes| / |gold_nodes ∪ pred_nodes|

Edge Jaccard: soft Jaccard using partial-credit type similarity (see tcgc/ontology/edges.py). Near-miss edge types (e.g., ASSERTED vs ACKNOWLEDGED) receive partial credit (0.5) rather than 0.

Worked example: Gold has 2 nodes and 1 edge. Pred has both nodes (node_j = 1.0) but the edge type is ACKNOWLEDGED instead of ASSERTED (similarity = 0.5). Score = 0.4 × 1.0 + 0.6 × 0.5 = 0.70.

Edge cases: Empty gold + empty pred → 1.0. Empty pred with non-empty gold → 0.0. Node weights must sum to 1.0.

When it can mislead: Rewards node-heavy predictions that miss edge semantics. Watch components["edge_jaccard"] separately.

docs.title: provenance_f1
docs.text:
Formula: F1 over (primitive_id, provenance_ref) pairs.

precision = |pred_pairs ∩ gold_pairs| / |pred_pairs|
recall    = |pred_pairs ∩ gold_pairs| / |gold_pairs|
F1        = 2 × P × R / (P + R)

Worked example: Gold has 3 (id, ref) pairs. Pred has 3 pairs: 2 matching, 1 wrong. P = 2/3, R = 2/3, F1 = 0.667.

Edge cases: Span refs are matched as prefixes (doc1:spans:1 resolves if doc1 is in inputs). Future items may use character-span IoU.

When it can mislead: Does not penalize citing a real span for the wrong primitive.

docs.title: kendall_tau
docs.text:
Formula: Kendall's τ-b, normalized to [0, 1].

score = (τ + 1) / 2

Missing gold events in pred receive worst-case rank. NaN → 0.0 with a diagnostic note.

Worked example: Gold order [e1, e2, e3, e4]. Pred reverses to [e4, e3, e2, e1]. τ = -1.0, score = 0.0.

Edge cases: Single-element order → always 1.0. All events missing → 0.0.

When it can mislead: Robust to small local swaps; a single large transposition can dominate.

docs.title: contradiction_pair_f1
docs.text:
Formula: Materiality-weighted F1 over unordered {claim_a, claim_b} pairs.

material pair weight = 1.0
cosmetic pair weight = 0.25

Pair order is ignored: {c1, c2} equals {c2, c1}.

Worked example: Gold has 1 material + 1 cosmetic pair. Pred finds only the material pair. Weighted recall = 1.0 / (1.0 + 0.25) = 0.80. Precision = 1.0. F1 = 0.889.

Edge cases: Empty gold + empty pred → 1.0. components["f1_unweighted"] always reported alongside weighted F1.

When it can mislead: A system that finds only material pairs scores higher than one that finds all pairs including cosmetic ones with FPs.

docs.title: llm_judge_anchored
docs.text:
Formula: LLM judge score calibrated via isotonic regression on 20 anchor triples.

Gated behind TCGC_RUN_API=1. Returns 0.0 with a "skipped" note otherwise.

Known failure modes (Zheng et al. 2023): Position bias, verbosity bias, self-preference bias.

Calibration: Raw LLM score is passed through isotonic regression fitted on tcgc/scoring/anchors/v0.1.jsonl (20 {raw, human_score} pairs). This monotonically maps raw scores toward human-annotated values.

When it can mislead: Calibration is only as good as the anchor set. Small anchor sets (n=20) have high variance. Do not treat calibrated scores as ground truth; report alongside human spot-checks.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text: Six open questions the TCGC was designed to surface. These are active areas of investigation.

docs.title: 1. Does position/interest separation generalize across cultures?
docs.text:
The Fisher/Ury distinction between positions and interests is well-established in Western negotiation theory. It is unclear whether this distinction maps cleanly onto conflict structures from non-Western cultural contexts.

Current thinking: We are collecting items from peace-process and diplomatic domains with non-Western actors to test this. The interest-extraction task type is the primary probe.

docs.title: 2. How much does provenance granularity matter for downstream reasoning?
docs.text:
TCGC requires every primitive to cite a source span. But spans can range from a message-level id to a character-level offset. Does finer-grained provenance improve downstream conflict reasoning, or is document-level attribution sufficient?

Current thinking: v0.1 uses message/document ids. v0.2 will add character-span items to test this directly via the provenance_f1 IoU scoring path.

docs.title: 3. Can LLMs reliably separate commitment from aspiration?
docs.text:
The commitment-tracking task requires distinguishing a genuine commitment ("I'll own it") from an aspiration ("we should try to"). This is a key failure mode in conflict analysis.

Current thinking: Preliminary runs suggest GPT-4-class models confuse these ~30% of the time. The COMMITS_TO vs ASSERTED partial-credit similarity (0.5) is designed to surface this.

docs.title: 4. Is narrative drift detectable without temporal metadata?
docs.text:
tcgc-0002 strips dates explicitly. It is unknown whether LLMs can reconstruct temporal order from causal language alone, or whether they rely on date markers.

Current thinking: The kendall_tau metric on date-stripped items is the cleanest probe. Early results suggest significant degradation when dates are removed.

docs.title: 5. What is the right materiality threshold for contradictions?
docs.text:
The contradiction-detection task classifies pairs as material or cosmetic. The current threshold (1.0 vs 0.25 weight) is a judgment call. A miscalibrated threshold distorts leaderboard rankings.

Current thinking: We will run a calibration study on the full corpus to fit the weight empirically from annotator materiality judgments.

docs.title: 6. How do graph-overlap scores correlate with downstream task performance?
docs.text:
TCGC scores a typed subgraph — but does a higher graph_overlap score actually predict better performance on downstream conflict-analysis tasks (e.g., mediation support, risk assessment)?

Current thinking: This requires a downstream task paired study. The TACITUS Dialectica reference system will provide the first data point.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get running in 5 minutes.

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/sargonxg/TCGC_TACITUS-Conflict-Grammar-Corpus_BENCHMARK
cd TCGC_TACITUS-Conflict-Grammar-Corpus_BENCHMARK
pip install -e '.[dev]'

docs.title: 2. Validate the sample items
docs.text:
tcgc validate items/v0.1-sample/

Expected output: 5 items, 0 errors, 0 warnings.

docs.title: 3. Print the schema
docs.text: tcgc schema --version v0.1 | python -m json.tool | head -30

docs.title: 4. Run an identity baseline (gold = prediction)
docs.text:
import json
from pathlib import Path

items_dir = Path("items/v0.1-sample")
preds = []
for p in sorted(items_dir.glob("tcgc-*.json")):
    item = json.loads(p.read_text())
    pred = {"id": item["id"]}
    pred.update(item["gold"])
    preds.append(json.dumps(pred))

Path("predictions.jsonl").write_text("\n".join(preds))

tcgc score predictions.jsonl items/v0.1-sample/ --out scores.json
tcgc report scores.json

Expected: 4/5 scorers return 1.0; llm_judge_anchored returns 0.0 with a "skipped" note (set TCGC_RUN_API=1 to enable).

docs.title: 5. Generate a system card
docs.text:
tcgc card scores.json --out SYSTEM_CARD.md

Edit SYSTEM_CARD.md and you're ready to submit.

docs.title: Q4 2026 — Dataset paper
docs.text: Full TCGC corpus paper submitted to a top-tier NLP venue. The paper covers: annotation methodology, ontology design decisions, IAA analysis, baseline system results, and open questions.

docs.title: Q1 2027 — OAG methodology
docs.text: Publication of the Ontology-Anchored Generation (OAG) methodology — the system used to bootstrap TCGC item generation from the Conflict Grammar. This will include tooling for researchers to extend the corpus in new domains.

docs.title: Q2 2027 — TCGC v2
docs.text:
• Extended corpus: ~1,000+ items
  • New task types: escalation-prediction, actor-alignment-shift
  • Character-span provenance (IoU scoring)
  • Multi-turn negotiation items
  • Non-English items (pilot)

docs.title: Ongoing
docs.text:
• Leaderboard updates as submissions are received
  • Ontology extensions via community proposal process (Tier 04)
  • Full-corpus access via DUA (email hello@tacitus.me)

docs.title: Submission Guide
docs.text: Submit your system's predictions to the TCGC leaderboard via a GitHub Pull Request.

docs.title: 1. Fork the repository
docs.text: Fork sargonxg/TCGC_TACITUS-Conflict-Grammar-Corpus_BENCHMARK and clone your fork.

docs.title: 2. Run your system over the sample items
docs.text:
pip install -e '.[dev]'
# Run your system — output must be a JSONL file where each line has:
# { "id": "tcgc-NNNN", ...gold-shaped fields... }

docs.title: 3. Score locally
docs.text:
tcgc score your_predictions.jsonl items/v0.1-sample/ --out scores.json
tcgc report scores.json

docs.title: 4. Create your submission directory
docs.text:
submissions/
  <your-system-name>/
    <YYYY-MM-DD>/
      predictions.jsonl     # your model outputs
      SYSTEM_CARD.md        # generated by: tcgc card scores.json
      submission.json       # metadata (see schema below)

docs.title: 5. Fill in submission.json
docs.text:
{
  "system_name": "My System v1.0",
  "system_version": "1.0.0",
  "contact_email": "you@example.com",
  "predictions_path": "submissions/my-system/2026-04-18/predictions.jsonl",
  "system_card_path": "submissions/my-system/2026-04-18/SYSTEM_CARD.md",
  "scorer_commit": "abc1234"
}

Get the scorer commit hash: git rev-parse --short HEAD

docs.title: 6. Open a Pull Request
docs.text:
Push your branch and open a PR against main. The leaderboard CI will:

  1. Validate submission.json against schema/submission-v0.1.json
  2. Re-run tcgc score on your predictions
  3. Post the per-task-type results as a PR comment

docs.title: 7. Merge and leaderboard update
docs.text: Once the PR is merged, results appear in leaderboard/v0.1-sample.json.

docs.title: Alternatively: email submission
docs.text: Send predictions.jsonl and SYSTEM_CARD.md to hello@tacitus.me. Include system name, version, and contact.

docs.title: Task Types
docs.text: 14 task types. Each maps to exactly one metric — mismatches are a validation error.

docs.title: 1. actor-resolution → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Identify and disambiguate all parties in the input who can hold interests or make claims. Resolve aliases and co-references to a canonical actor set.

Input shape: Messages, documents, or transcript. May include an actors hint list.

Output shape: Primitives of type actor with provenance bindings; edges where actors are in relationships.

Failure modes: Splitting one actor into two; merging two distinct actors; missing implicit actors (e.g., "the board" as a collective).

Mini-example: Input mentions "the Chair", "Director Park", and "she". A correct resolution collapses "she" → "Director Park".

docs.title: 2. claim-extraction → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Extract every asserted, denied, or acknowledged factual or evaluative statement, attributed to the speaker making it.

Input shape: Any input modality.

Output shape: Primitives of type claim; edges of type ASSERTED, DENIED, ACKNOWLEDGED, ACKNOWLEDGED_AMBIGUOUSLY.

Failure modes: Extracting background facts as claims; missing hedged claims; incorrect attribution.

docs.title: 3. interest-extraction → llm_judge_anchored
docs.text:
Definition: Extract the underlying goals and needs motivating each actor, distinct from their stated positions (Fisher/Ury distinction).

Input shape: Transcript or messages where positions are stated.

Output shape: Primitives of type interest; edges of type HOLDS_INTEREST.

Failure modes: Restating positions as interests; hallucinating interests not supported by text; missing latent interests.

docs.title: 4. constraint-extraction → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Identify structural rules, norms, or limits that bound feasible outcomes for one or more actors.

Output shape: Primitives of type constraint; edges of type CONSTRAINED_BY.

docs.title: 5. leverage-mapping → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Map resources, dependencies, or capabilities that shift bargaining power between actors.

Output shape: Primitives of type leverage; edges of type LEVERAGES.

docs.title: 6. commitment-tracking → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Identify promises of future action, who made them, whether they were acknowledged or contested, and their current status.

Output shape: Primitives of type commitment with subject, deadline, status fields; edges of type COMMITS_TO, ASSERTED, ACKNOWLEDGED_AMBIGUOUSLY, DENIES_SCOPE, REVOKES.

Failure modes: Confusing an aspiration with a commitment; missing a revocation; incorrect status.

docs.title: 7. event-ordering → kendall_tau
docs.text:
Definition: Reconstruct the chronological sequence of discrete events mentioned in the input, even when dates have been stripped.

Output shape: Primitives of type event; edges of type PRECEDES; a top-level order array.

Failure modes: Reversing causal chains; treating simultaneous events as sequential.

docs.title: 8. narrative-drift → llm_judge_anchored
docs.text:
Definition: Detect when an actor's framing of events shifts across time or documents, and characterize the direction and magnitude of the shift.

Output shape: Primitives of type narrative; edges of type FRAMES; temporal ordering of narrative versions.

docs.title: 9. causal-chain → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Reconstruct the causal pathway linking a trigger event to an outcome, identifying intermediate causes.

Output shape: Primitives of type event; edges of type CAUSES.

docs.title: 10. contradiction-detection → contradiction_pair_f1
docs.text:
Definition: Identify pairs of claims that contradict each other, and classify each pair as material (affects outcomes) or cosmetic (procedural/minor).

Output shape: contradictions array with claim_a, claim_b, materiality, rationale.

Failure modes: Flagging tension as contradiction; missing implicit contradictions; miscategorizing materiality.

docs.title: 11. provenance-attribution → provenance_f1
docs.text:
Definition: Extract every attributable claim and bind each to its exact source span in the input document.

Output shape: Primitives with provenance refs pointing to specific document ids or span identifiers.

Failure modes: Orphan provenance (ref not in input); merging claims from different sources.

docs.title: 12. commitment-claim-mismatch → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Identify cases where an actor's stated commitment contradicts their earlier or later claims.

Output shape: Commitment primitive + claim primitives + edges showing the mismatch.

docs.title: 13. position-interest-separation → llm_judge_anchored
docs.text:
Definition: For each actor, cleanly separate stated positions from inferred underlying interests.

Output shape: Both claim (position) and interest primitives for each actor; edges ASSERTED (positions) and HOLDS_INTEREST (interests).

docs.title: 14. cross-document-synthesis → graph_overlap
docs.text:
Definition: Synthesize a unified conflict graph from multiple documents that may contradict or complement each other.

Output shape: Full ACO subgraph integrating all documents; edges include CONTRADICTS, SUPPORTS, CITES.
